title: The Briefing Room
setup:
You're presenting the Meridian brief to a room that includes two managing directors you've never met, Grace, Dana, and three people from the client side. Thirty minutes, your deck, your call.

Five minutes in, one of the MDs — Harlow, who hasn't spoken until now — raises his hand and interrupts. He thinks your first assumption is wrong. He doesn't phrase it as a question.

The room goes very still. Dana is watching.

options.label: Defend the assumption with data
options.text: You acknowledge his concern, then walk through the evidence that supports the assumption — three sources, one counter-example you've already accounted for. You don't yield.
options.innerMonologue: You held the line in a room where holding lines is expensive. Whether you were right is a separate question from whether you should have done it.

options.label: Acknowledge the challenge and offer to revisit
options.text: You say he's raised something worth examining properly. You propose that you incorporate this line of questioning into a revised section and send it to the room within 48 hours.
options.innerMonologue: You bought the room peace and yourself 48 hours. Dana will want to know if you actually think the assumption holds.

options.label: Ask him to restate his concern more specifically
options.text: You say: 'Can you help me understand exactly which part of the assumption you think doesn't hold?' You wait. He narrows it. His actual objection is much smaller than his opening implied.
options.innerMonologue: Making him be specific was the right move. The room noticed you made him be specific.

options.label: Concede the point and adjust the frame
options.text: You say he's right that the assumption is contestable and reframe the brief: the conclusion holds even under the weaker assumption. You continue from there.
options.innerMonologue: You gave him the point and kept the room. The conclusion now rests on weaker ground. That may matter later.

designNotes: High-pressure room test. Option A is the full Cortés play — high nerve, potentially alienates. Option C is the sophisticated read — makes him be specific — which is realistically the strongest move. Dana's watching for how the player handles authority.
